data.title: A Man Complains He Pays For Every Date With His Girlfriend, Even 'In This Economy.' Dave Ramsey Responds, 'Is She A Money-Hungry Gold Digger?'
data.description:
Paying for dinner on a date is one thing. Paying for everything in a relationship is another.

Oscar from Canada says that’s exactly the situation he found himself in after five months of dating his girlfriend. On a recent episode of “The Ramsey Show,” he said he has covered nearly every expense, and it’s starting to bother him, especially given today’s economic pressures.

Cultural Expectations Or A Problem?

Oscar, who is originally from Iran, explained that his girlfriend, who has Polish roots but grew up in Canada, told him early on that in her culture men typically pay for dates.

While he said he has some traditional views himself, the arrangement started to feel unbalanced.

Don't Miss:

“Since day one, I’ve been paying for restaurants, going out, activities, snacks and everything,” he said. “I’m traditional sort of, but at the same time, I don’t see the point of man paying for everything, especially in this economy.”

The concern grew when the couple began discussing a potential trip to Europe. According to Oscar, his girlfriend suggested he pay for the flights and hotel while she would cover food during the trip.

Given that flights and hotels make up most of the cost of a trip, Oscar said it would result in him covering about 85% to 90% of the total expense.

Personal finance expert Dave Ramsey first approached the situation from a broader relationship perspective. In his view, the strongest relationships aren’t about tracking who pays for what.

“The marriages that I know, that I consider some of the best marriages on the planet, are where each of the people involved make the relationship about how much they can serve the other person,” Ramsey said. “How good can I be to you? Instead of what am I getting out of this?”

Trending: Think you're saving enough for your kids? You might be dangerously off — see why

The Real Red Flag

Ramsey acknowledged that cultural expectations can play a role in dating habits. He pointed out that in the Southern U.S., where he grew up, it was common for men to pay for everything.

“I’m an old southern redneck,” Ramsey said. “In our world, 100% the guy pays for everything. Period. That’s called chivalry in our world.”

But he also said there is a clear line between generosity and entitlement.

“The last thing I want is a high-maintenance princess that’s entitled,” Ramsey said. “That’s cray cray, I’m going to avoid that woman like the plague.”

At one point, Ramsey asked Oscar directly: “Is she a money-hungry gold digger?”

Oscar answered that he believed she was “money hungry.” Even so, Ramsey later said he didn’t necessarily hear clear signs of a gold digger based on the overall description. Instead, he suggested the larger issue might be whether the couple shares the same values around money.

“If you can’t get aligned on the handling of money and the value of money in this relationship, that is a red flag for any relationship,” Ramsey said.

Conversations about money can be uncomfortable, especially early in a relationship. Platforms like Money Pickle connect users with fiduciary financial advisors who can help couples talk through budgeting, financial goals and spending habits so they can build a plan that works for both partners.

Co-host George Kamel suggested a practical way to test whether spending expectations were becoming a problem. He recommended choosing simpler, low-cost dates and seeing how the girlfriend responds.

“Set up the boundaries earlier on,” Kamel said. “Say, ‘Hey, you want to just take a walk in the park?'”

In the end, Ramsey said the biggest concern isn’t who pays for dinner. The real issue is whether both people see money the same way.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Palantir Shows AI Is The New Battlefield, Puts Defense Tech ETFs In The Spotlight
data.description:
Artificial intelligence (AI) is quickly becoming a central battlefield technology, and ETFs focused on defense and AI infrastructure may be a beneficiary of the emerging trend.

Speaking with CNBC, Karp emphasized the increasing importance of AI technology in military operations and intelligence analysis. He cited the example of the US Department of Defense's AI-powered surveillance technology called Project Maven.

The technology uses machine learning algorithms to analyze satellite imagery and battlefield intelligence to identify military targets. According to sources, the technology has now integrated AI models developed by AI technology firm called Anthropic. The firm's chatbot technology called Claude is said to have been integrated into some defense technology tools.

The technology is now being highlighted in the escalating conflict between the US and Iran. The conflict is a reminder that modern warfare is not just about traditional military technology.

Defense ETFs Are Becoming AI Plays

But newer strategies such as the Global X Defense Tech ETF (NYSE:SHLD) focus more directly on emerging defense technologies including autonomous systems, cybersecurity and artificial intelligence.

Shares of Palantir have surged alongside the growing demand for defense-related AI tools. The stock is up almost 15% this month even as the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite has declined 2%.

The AI Arms Race

Industry experts are increasingly discussing the possibility that the next geopolitical arms race will be in AI. AI platforms can process vast amounts of data from different forms of intelligence, such as satellite and communication data.

Analysts say that as geopolitical tensions rise and governments accelerate spending on defense technology, ETFs that focus on defense innovation and AI infrastructure could attract renewed interest. The future of defense investing could be less about tanks and planes and more about algorithms.

data.title: The Average Person Wants Passive Income, But Keeps Thinking About Money The Same Way. 'Start By Unlearning Everything'
data.description:
Many people like the idea of passive income. After all, the concept is simple: money coming in even when you are not working. But a recent Reddit discussion shows that many people misunderstand how it actually works.

“The average person has to start by unlearning everything they think they know about money and replace it with how money works for you,” one commenter summed up the biggest issue with this type of income. In other words, passive income is rarely magic or instant. It is usually the result of years of effort, saving, and investing.

Start With A Regular Job

One point came up again and again in the discussion: passive income usually starts with regular income. Most people first make money through a job or a business, then use some of that money to buy things that can start bringing in income on their own.

Don't Miss: Most founders obsess over the wrong hires. See the 5 startup roles that actually determine whether a company scales or stalls.

As one person explained, “Work hard to make a lot of money, then buy passive income.” That means the first step is often building a stable career, saving money, and avoiding lifestyle inflation.

“Find a stable job,” another commenter described a realistic path many people follow. “Do something relevant on the side. Don't spend too much money. Invest the money you earn in the market.” Over time, those investments can grow into meaningful income streams.

For many wealth builders, the three most common sources of income are straightforward: Real estate, investments and business income. These aren’t always passive in the beginning, but they can become increasingly hands-off over time.

Actionable Ways To Start Building Passive Income

Although the phrase “multiple income streams” can sound intimidating, many commenters pointed out that ordinary people can start small.

Trending: Think you're saving enough for your kids? You might be dangerously off — see why

Investing is one of the most accessible options. Consistently buying index funds or dividend-paying stocks allows people to benefit from long-term growth and compounding returns. Even modest monthly investments can grow significantly over time.

Real estate was another commonly mentioned strategy. Some suggested “house hacking,” which involves buying a property and renting out spare rooms or additional units. 

“My son bought a triplex when he made his first property purchase,” one person gave an example. “The rent from 2 of the apts makes the payment for all 3.” Over time, rising rents and property appreciation can strengthen financial stability.

Another option people mentioned is digital products. For example, you create an online course, a template, or an ebook. It takes work at the beginning, but once it is created, people can keep buying it later without much additional effort.

Another key lesson from the discussion is patience. Many income streams take years to become meaningful. One commenter said it took four years before any side income started to feel consistent.

Wealth Builds Gradually, Not Overnight

People often assume wealthy individuals started with multiple income streams, but that is rarely the case.

“The foundation is most often first built with active income,” one commenter wrote. After that, investments and assets gradually stack on top of the original source of earnings.

If you're serious about turning small side hustles into real passive income, platforms like Money Pickle can connect you with fiduciary financial advisors. They help map out a plan, prioritize your savings, and identify investments that fit your goals — so your money actually works for you over time.

Others added that even small streams can change how people think about money. Passive income might not make you wealthy right away, but it makes you unconcerned about everyday expenses that keep rising. That sense of financial breathing room can create opportunities to pursue new ventures.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Benzinga Bulls And Bears: Kodak, Tesla, Adobe — And Iran War Keeps Markets Volatile
data.description:
Benzinga examined the prospects for many investors' favorite stocks over the last week — here's a look at some of our top stories.

At the same time, the energy-driven inflation shock is beginning to spill into housing markets. U.S. mortgage rates climbed to about 6.11%, their highest level in several weeks, as geopolitical tensions unsettled bond markets and pushed Treasury yields higher. The rise in borrowing costs complicates the spring homebuying season, reinforcing concerns that the oil shock could ripple beyond commodities and equities into broader economic activity.

Benzinga provides daily reports on the stocks most popular with investors. Here are a few of this past week's most bullish and bearish posts that are worth another look.

data.title: OWL, HIMS, TSLA And More: 5 Stocks Investors Couldn't Stop Buzzing About This Week
data.description:
Retail investors talked up five hot stocks this week (March 9 to March 13) on X and Reddit's r/WallStreetBets, driven by retail hype, earnings, AI buzz, and corporate news flow.

Oracle

  • Some retail investors were questioning other traders about buying ORCL after its post-earnings jump.
  • The stock had a 52-week range of $118.86 to $345.72, trading around $159 to $162 per share, as of the publication of this article. It rose 5.48% over the year and fell 45.53% over the last six months.
  • ORCL had a weaker price trend in the short, medium, and long term, with a poor value ranking, as per Benzinga's Edge Stock Rankings.

Hims & Hers

  • Some retail investors were mocking the short sellers on HIMS after the resolution of the feud with NVO.
  • The stock had a 52-week range of $13.74 to $70.43, trading around $23 to $25 per share, as of the publication of this article. It declined by 29.47% over the year and 57.05% in the last six months.
  • HIMS had a weaker price trend in the medium and long terms but a strong trend in the short term, with a moderate growth ranking as per Benzinga's Edge Stock Rankings.

Blue Owl Capital

  • Some retail investors were rejoicing that some private credit companies would stumble if a financial crisis occurred.
  • The stock had a 52-week range of $8.60 to $21.88, trading around $8 to $10 per share, as of the publication of this article. It declined 54.61% over the year and 54.08% in the last six months.
  • Benzinga's Edge Stock Rankings showed that OWL had a weak price trend in the short, medium, and long terms, with a poor value ranking.

Strategy

  • Some retail investors were nervous ahead of MSTR’s call options expiry.
  • The stock had a 52-week range of $104.17 to $457.22, trading around $137 to $142 per share, as of the publication of this article. It was down 47.69% over the year and 58.56% over the last six months.
  • MSTR maintains a weaker price trend over the short, medium, and long terms, as per Benzinga's Edge Stock Rankings.

Tesla

  • An investor praised TSLA’s Thursday decline, saying the world was “healing.”
  • The stock had a 52-week range of $214.25 to $498.83, trading around $395 to $400 per share, as of the publication of this article. It advanced by 59.22% over the year and down 0.23% over the last six months.
  • According to Benzinga's Edge Stock Rankings, TSLA was maintaining a weak price trend over the short and medium terms but a strong trend in the long term, with a moderate quality ranking.

Retail focus blended meme-driven narrative with earnings outlook and corporate news flow, as the S&P 500, Dow Jones, and Nasdaq witnessed mixed market action during the week.

Image via Shutterstock
